front: Sculpture: Depicts a camera-toting man in a Hawaiian shirt standing next to his wife (1970)
Sculpture: Overweight woman with hair curlers, varicose veins, and a shopping cart; created using lifecasting
Sculpture: A black janitorial worker, made with resin and fiberglass
Sculptor: Hyperrealist who used resin, fiberglass, and lifecasting to create uncannily realistic sculptures of everyday Americans
Sculptor: Part of the hyperrealism movement; contrasted with George Segal, who made white plaster figures at a diner, while this artist made fully realistic ones
